title: Recognising swine dysentery and serious pig scours
subtitle: How to tell routine loose droppings from infectious diarrhoea that needs veterinary attention.
excerpt: Not all pig scours are equal. Loose droppings from a diet change or mild stomach upset resolve without intervention. Swine dysentery, proliferative enteropathy, and salmonellosis are infectious diarrhoeas that spread through a herd and can kill pigs rapidly without antibiotic treatment. The practical skill is knowing which presentation is which.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003), gov.uk. Pig Veterinary Society: enteric disease factsheets. Merck Veterinary Manual: swine dysentery, proliferative enteropathy in pigs.

glossaryTerms.definition: A severe mucohemorrhagic diarrhoea caused by Brachyspira hyodysenteriae. Affects growing pigs and adults. The characteristic sign is blood and mucus in the faeces, with a loose, dark, or tarry consistency. Spreads rapidly through a herd; can persist on a farm for years once established. Notifiable to the vet; requires prescription antibiotic treatment.

An infection caused by the intracellular bacterium Lawsonia intracellularis, affecting the small intestine. Two presentations: chronic, where growing pigs lose condition and have loose brownish droppings without blood; and acute haemorrhagic, where older pigs pass large amounts of blood and may be found dead without prior signs. Treated with in-water antibiotics under prescription.

Infection with Salmonella Typhimurium or S. Choleraesuis in pigs, causing variable presentations from subclinical shedding to acute yellow watery diarrhoea with high mortality. A zoonotic disease: pigs can shed without showing symptoms, and handlers should treat biosecurity accordingly.

content.text: Some loose pig droppings need no response. Others are the start of an infectious outbreak that will spread to every pig on the unit within days. The distinction is not always obvious in the first 24 hours, but knowing what to look for lets you call a vet at the right moment rather than too late.
Routine loose droppings versus infectious diarrhoea

Routine digestive looseness in pigs is common: a change of feed, a sudden increase in fresh greens or root vegetables, wet conditions, or mild heat stress can produce loose droppings in otherwise healthy pigs. These pigs are still eating, moving normally, and the loose droppings affect one or two animals rather than the whole group. Recovery within 24 to 48 hours without other signs is reassuring.
Call a vet when more than one pig is affected and the condition is getting worse, the droppings contain blood, a pig stops eating, or any pig has died suddenly.
Swine dysentery
: blood and grey mucus in faeces; foul smell; pigs hunched and reluctant to move; affects growing pigs and adults. Spreads through the group.
Proliferative enteropathy (chronic)
: loose, brown or grey droppings without blood; pigs fall behind in growth and condition while still eating; no high mortality.
Proliferative enteropathy (acute)
: sudden death or a pig found pale and passing large volumes of dark blood; typical in gilts or sows. A dead pig found pale in a puddle of blood is acute haemorrhagic PE until proven otherwise.
Salmonellosis
: profuse yellow watery diarrhoea; high temperature; rapid deterioration in severely affected pigs; sometimes purple-red skin discolouration on ears and belly in septicaemic cases.
Swine dysentery
Of the infectious scours,
swine dysentery

is the one most likely to come in with a bought-in pig. Brachyspira can stay in a recovered pig's gut with no signs; the carrier infects resident stock when stressed or mixed. Quarantine all incoming pigs for three weeks before mixing them with your stock.
Proliferative enteropathy
The chronic form of
proliferative enteropathy

is insidious. Pigs grow more slowly, feed conversion worsens, but no pig seems obviously ill. The acute haemorrhagic form is dramatic and alarming. Both forms respond to prescription antibiotics. Your vet can confirm the diagnosis by post-mortem examination or faecal PCR.
Biosecurity and zoonotic risk

All serious pig diarrhoeas require heightened biosecurity during the outbreak: separate your footwear used in the pig area, wash hands before and after contact, and do not move pigs between pens or off the unit until your vet has confirmed the cause.
Salmonellosis in pigs
is zoonotic. Handlers should wear gloves and avoid contact with faeces during an active outbreak.
